epigraph.text:
Well done, good and faithful servant! You have 
been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many 
things.

content.text:
This chapter has taken us on a journey through the world of 
quadrilaterals, from the most general to the most specialized. We 
began with polygons and their angle sums, then focused on 
parallelograms and their remarkable properties. We explored the 
special parallelograms—rectangles, rhombi, and squares—and 
finally examined trapezoids and kites, quadrilaterals that are 
not parallelograms but have their own elegant characteristics.

The key to mastering this material is understanding the 
*hierarchy* of quadrilaterals: how each type inherits 
properties from more general types while adding its own special 
characteristics.

items: A polygon with $n$ sides has interior angle sum: $(n-2) \cdot 180°$
Each interior angle of a regular $n$-gon: $\displaystyle\frac{(n-2) \cdot 180°}{n}$
The sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon is always $360°$
Each exterior angle of a regular $n$-gon: $\displaystyle\frac{360°}{n}$
Regular polygons have all sides congruent and all angles congruent
**Definition:** Both pairs of opposite sides parallel

**Properties:**
 itemize
Opposite sides congruent
Opposite angles congruent
Consecutive angles supplementary

Diagonals bisect each other
 itemize

**Ways to prove a quadrilateral is a parallelogram:**
 enumerate
Both pairs of opposite sides parallel (definition)
Both pairs of opposite sides congruent
Both pairs of opposite angles congruent
One pair of opposite sides both parallel AND congruent

Diagonals bisect each other
 enumerate

rows: Both a rectangle AND a rhombus
Diagonals are congruent, perpendicular, and bisect vertex angles

headers: Exactly one pair of parallel sides (bases)
rows: Two pairs of consecutive congruent sides
One pair of opposite angles congruent

items: Find the interior angle sum of a hexagon.
Find each interior angle of a regular decagon.
A polygon has interior angle sum $1980°$. How many sides?

List four conditions that prove a quadrilateral is a 
 parallelogram.

In $\square JKLM$, $JK = 17$ and $m\angle J = 72°$. Find 
 $LM$ and $m\angle L$.
In $\square ABCD$, diagonals meet at $E$. If $AE = 9$, find $AC$.
The diagonals of a rhombus are 16 and 30. Find the perimeter.

What type of quadrilateral has exactly one pair of parallel 
 sides and congruent diagonals?

In kite $PQRS$ with $\overline{PQ} \cong \overline{PS}$, 
 which angles are congruent?
A trapezoid has bases 12 and 20, with height 8. Find its area.

Prove: If the diagonals of a parallelogram are congruent, 
 it is a rectangle.

In quadrilateral $ABCD$, $A(0,0)$, $B(5,0)$, $C(8,4)$, $D(3,4)$. 
 Determine if $ABCD$ is a parallelogram, and if so, identify what 
 special type (if any).

content.text:
Quadrilaterals form a hierarchy of increasing specialization. At 
the top sits the general quadrilateral, constrained only by having 
four sides. Adding the requirement that opposite sides be parallel 
creates the parallelogram, with its elegant properties of congruence 
and diagonal bisection. Adding right angles gives us the rectangle; 
adding equal sides gives us the rhombus. Adding both creates the 
square—the most constrained, most symmetric, and most property-rich 
of all quadrilaterals.

This hierarchical thinking—organizing objects by their properties 
and understanding how specialization adds new characteristics—is 
fundamental to mathematical reasoning. When we prove something about 
parallelograms, that proof automatically applies to rectangles, 
rhombi, and squares. Understanding that a square is a special 
rectangle helps us know which theorems apply without needing to 
prove each one separately.

The trapezoid and kite remind us that not everything fits neatly 
into the parallelogram family. These quadrilaterals sacrifice 
some properties (like having both pairs of opposite sides parallel) 
but gain others (like the elegant midsegment theorem for trapezoids 
or the perpendicular diagonals of kites).

The quadrilateral hierarchy organizes shapes 
from general to specific, with each level adding constraints that 
yield new properties. Consider how this principle of hierarchical 
classification appears in other areas—in biology's taxonomy, in 
the organization of society, or in theological categories. What 
does the geometric hierarchy teach us about the value of both 
generality and specialization?

As you continue in geometry, you will find that the properties 
mastered in this chapter—parallel lines, congruent segments, 
angle relationships, and diagonal behavior—appear again and 
again. The parallelogram, in particular, is a workhorse of 
geometric proof, appearing in constructions, coordinate geometry, 
and even vector mathematics. Master these quadrilaterals, and you 
have mastered tools that will serve you throughout your mathematical 
journey.
These problems review concepts from earlier chapters.

marginNotes.text:
By wisdom a house is built, and 
through understanding it is established; through knowledge its 
rooms are filled with rare and beautiful treasures.

For the Lord gives wisdom; from his 
mouth come knowledge and understanding.
